content:
12 min read
Just now

--

We used to care about profitability. Somewhere along the way, we stopped.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I remember a time when I used to look at startups by one thing: profitability. And just like me, many others used to do the same. That’s how people invested, that’s how they decided whether a company was actually worth betting on, and honestly, that’s also how students decided where they wanted to work. If a company was profitable, there was a certain confidence around it. It meant that somebody somewhere was paying for the product, that there was real demand, and that the business wasn’t just surviving on investor money. Back then, conversations revolved around customers, revenue, sustainability, and whether the company could stand on its own feet.
Profitability isn’t the first thing people ask about anymore. Instead, we have somehow normalised another metric, cash burn. Today, the discussion is around how much money you’re willing to spend, how aggressively you’re investing in the future, and whether you can keep your shareholders convinced that all of this spending is eventually going to pay off. Somewhere along the way, burning money stopped looking like a weakness and started looking like ambition. And if there is one industry where this change has been the most visible, it has to be AI.
ChatGPT changed the incentives for an entire industry.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When ChatGPT came around, I don’t think many of us realized what was actually happening behind the scenes. Sure, we all saw an incredibly capable chatbot that could write code, answer questions, summarize documents, and do things that previously felt impossible. But what happened in the months after that was much bigger than just another product launch.
Almost overnight, OpenAI became one of the most valuable AI companies in the world. More importantly, for the first time in years, people genuinely questioned whether Google would continue to be the only place they needed to search for information. Users loved it, investors loved it even more, and before anyone realized it, everybody wanted to jump onto the AI train.
Once one company starts becoming incredibly valuable because of a new technology, the rest of the industry doesn’t simply sit back and watch. Within months, almost every major company had an AI announcement. Google launched Gemini across its products, Microsoft integrated Copilot into Windows, Office, and GitHub, Anthropic continued improving Claude, Meta introduced Meta AI (didn’t work though :) ), Perplexity started changing how people searched the web, and suddenly almost every SaaS company had an “AI-powered” feature somewhere on its homepage.
At that point, AI stopped being a competitive advantage and started becoming an expectation. If your company didn’t have an AI story, investors wanted to know what you were missing. The race wasn’t simply about building useful software anymore; it became a race to convince the market that your AI was going to define the future.
AI is expensive, isn’t it ?
AI isn’t cheap. Training models isn’t cheap, inference definitely isn’t cheap, building GPU clusters isn’t cheap, and hiring some of the smartest researchers in the world definitely isn’t cheap either. Every company wanted to ride the AI train in the premium coach while somehow buying the economy ticket, and that simply isn’t how this industry works.
Unlike traditional software where serving another user costs relatively little, AI products keep charging you every single time someone asks a question or generates an image. The more successful your product becomes, the larger your infrastructure bill becomes alongside it.
That’s exactly where the narrative started changing.
Instead of saying “We’re burning billions of dollars every year,” companies started saying something much easier for investors to digest — they’re investing. Buying GPUs became investment. Building clusters became investment. Hiring researchers became investment. And to be fair, a lot of it genuinely is. Every major technology shift has required companies willing to spend years building infrastructure before the returns eventually arrived.
But this also changed how we look at profitability. If almost every expense can be justified as an investment into the future, then profitability slowly becomes something you can always postpone. Maybe next year. Maybe after the next model. Maybe after inference costs become cheaper. Maybe after agents become mainstream. There is always another milestone waiting just around the corner.
When cash burn becomes the business strategy
Before you follow along, do read this one out so you understand why and where the terminology came is from.
This is also one of the biggest reasons why we’re seeing trillion-dollar valuations around AI companies today. Investors aren’t simply paying for today’s products; they’re paying for the possibility that one of these companies ends up defining the next decade of computing.
That naturally creates another expectation. If you’ve raised billions, the next announcement can’t simply be “Our chatbot got a little better.” It has to feel like the future, SCI-FI!
It has to convince investors that this spending is justified.
Keep pedalling..
The bigger your promises become, the bigger your funding round also becomes. That funding allows you to buy more GPUs, hire more researchers, spend more on training, and naturally your cash burn also increases. Once you’ve spent all that money, investors expect another breakthrough, which means you now have to make even bigger promises than before. Once you notice this cycle, it’s honestly difficult to unsee it because almost every major AI company is now operating somewhere within it.
I also think this is one of the biggest mindset shifts we’ve had over the last few years. I remember a time when hearing that a company was burning hundreds of millions of dollars would immediately make people question its business model. Today, hearing that an AI company is spending billions on GPUs almost sounds reassuring because we’ve collectively accepted the idea that this is what building the future looks like. We hear phrases like “They’re investing in infrastructure,” “They’re building the next generation of AI,” or “Inference costs will eventually come down,” and those explanations make sense because AI genuinely is expensive.
The more interesting question is whether we’ve slowly reached a point where cash burn itself has become normal, where profitability is no longer the immediate goal, and where selling the possibility of tomorrow has become just as important as building something useful today.
The AI demo economy
One thing I’ve noticed over the last couple of years is that we’ve quietly entered what I like to call the AI demo economy.
Every launch video looks magical. Every keynote makes you feel like the future has already arrived. Then the actual product ships.
Take Google’s original Gemini launch as an example. The launch video showed Gemini understanding drawings, recognising objects, following conversations, and responding almost instantly. It was one of the most impressive AI demos we’d seen. Later, Google clarified that the demo wasn’t a single uninterrupted interaction and that prompts had been shortened while latency had been edited for presentation. The capabilities themselves were real, but the presentation made the interaction feel much smoother than what users would actually experience.
Rabbit R1 became another example that many people remember. The idea itself was genuinely exciting a dedicated AI companion that could interact with different services and reduce our dependence on smartphones. But when reviewers finally received the hardware, the experience wasn’t anywhere close to what the marketing had led many people to expect. Features were incomplete, performance wasn’t consistent, and a lot of people simply ended up asking why they shouldn’t just use their phones instead.
We saw something similar with the Humane AI Pin. The company presented a future where your smartphone wouldn’t be the center of your digital life anymore. But after launch, reviewers pointed out issues around overheating, latency, battery life, and overall usability. Once again, the vision was significantly ahead of where the product had reached.
And honestly, I don’t think these are isolated examples. They’re symptoms of a much larger trend — when investors are funding the future, companies naturally start marketing the future.
Bigger promises become a necessity
Think about this from the company’s perspective.
If you’re spending billions every single year on GPUs, researchers, infrastructure, and training, can you really afford to launch a model that’s described as “slightly better than last year’s”? Probably not.
Every announcement now has to feel like a leap forward. Every benchmark has to prove that this model is smarter than the last one. Every press release has to make users feel like they’re one subscription away from the future. Because if you don’t make that promise, somebody else will.
This is why the AI race has become less about individual products and more about expectations. Every company knows that the next funding round, the next valuation, or even the next stock market reaction depends on convincing people that they’re still leading the race.
The pressure has never been this high
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One thing that makes AI different from many previous technology waves is that your product doesn’t stay impressive for very long.
A feature that’s groundbreaking today can become an API call six months later. A reasoning model that tops the leaderboard today can be overtaken by another release next month. An agent workflow that feels magical today might become an open-source project by the end of the year.
You’re competing against startups, trillion-dollar companies, open-source communities, researchers publishing papers every week, companies building custom AI chips, and people who are running increasingly capable models on their own hardware at home. The finish line keeps moving, and because the finish line keeps moving, companies have to keep spending.
Then DeepSeek happened…
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I remember when DeepSeek started making headlines because I don’t think people were only looking at another AI model. They were looking at what it represented.
Markets reacted, companies reacted, and even investors started asking uncomfortable questions about whether everyone had been spending efficiently. If another lab could build a capable reasoning model while changing the assumptions around cost and efficiency, then maybe the AI race wasn’t only about who could spend the most money anymore. Until then, many people assumed that throwing more GPUs and more money at the problem would naturally produce better AI. DeepSeek reminded everyone that efficiency also matters.
At the same time, we’re seeing more and more powerful open-source models coming out of Chinese AI labs and the broader open-source community. Honestly, one of my favorite things to see nowadays is opening Reddit and finding people running these models on their own home servers. A few years ago that would’ve sounded ridiculous. Today it’s becoming surprisingly common.
And that’s another kind of pressure commercial AI companies now have to deal with. If users can run increasingly capable models locally, why should they continue paying every month? Suddenly, companies aren’t just competing with OpenAI or Anthropic anymore. They’re competing with models that anybody can download, fine-tune, and run on their own hardware.
Benchmarks have quietly become the new marketing
Every major launch now comes with another graph, another leaderboard, another benchmark comparison, and another claim about being state-of-the-art.
Whether it’s SWE-bench, MMLU, HumanEval, GPQA, A
